data.title: Google fined $666M in German shopping suits - Bloomberg
data.description:
Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) was ordered to pay €573M ($666M) in damages to two German price-comparison websites in the wake of a European Union antitrust crackdown on the search engine giant’s abuse of market power, Bloomberg reported on Friday.

The report said that Axel Springer SE-owned Idealo, which initially sought €3.3B, was awarded €374M plus €91M in interest in a ruling at the Berlin Regional Court. Producto GmbH, another price-comparison service that sought €290M, was granted €89.7M plus €17.7M in interest.

"We are pleased that the court dismissed the majority of these exorbitant claims and require the claimants to bear most of the costs," Google reportedly said in a statement. "However, we disagree with the two rulings overall and will appeal."

The civil suits are linked to a 2017 decision by the European Commission to fine Google €2.4B for illegally leveraging its search dominance to give its shopping service an edge.

The EU decision unleashed a wave of so-called follow-on suits, which were delayed for years as Google appealed the EU fine. Then, last year, a tribunal confirmed that the company did violate antitrust laws—meaning EU-based plaintiffs no longer have to prove that in court, the report said.

According to the report, Idealo co-founder Albrecht von Sonntag welcomed the court's decision to hold Google accountable for 15 years of competition abuse but said he will appeal and try to recover "a bigger amount."

"We will continue to fight because market abuse must have consequences and must not become a lucrative business model worthwhile despite fines and damages payments," von Sonntag reportedly said.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: CDTX, AMAT, WBD, crypto stocks, and more
data.description:
Stock futures edged down on Friday, pressured by renewed concerns over slowing global growth and reduced odds of an additional U.S. Federal Reserve rate cut this year. 

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Cidara Therapeutics (CDTX) +104% - Shares surged after the Financial Times reported that Merck (MRK) is close to acquiring the biotech, which is developing a long-acting antibody to protect against the flu. A deal expected to come at a premium to Cidara’s $3.3B market cap could be announced as early as Friday if final negotiations don’t falter. Merck was reportedly competing with at least one other pharmaceutical bidder before its offer was selected, though the final purchase price remains unclear; the structure is expected to include upfront cash plus milestone-based payments tied to clinical progress.
  • Virgin Galactic (SPCE) +12% - Shares rose after the company beat top- and bottom-line estimates and narrowed its quarterly loss through lower operating expenses. Q4 revenue is expected to be about $300K, tied mainly to astronaut access fees, and free cash flow is projected to be between –$90M and –$100M. Management said spending will decline through Q3 2026 before rising ahead of commercial service in Q4 2026. CEO Michael Colglazier reaffirmed that the company remains on track to open its first sales tranche for future missions in Q1 2026 and reiterated its long-term model: with two SpaceShips capable of ~125 flights per year and ticket pricing at $600K per seat, Virgin Galactic expects roughly $450M in annual revenue and about $100M in adjusted EBITDA at scale.
  • Quantum Computing (QUBT) +10% - Shares surged ahead of its Q3 earnings report scheduled for release after market close on Friday, November 14th. Analyst consensus expects an EPS of -$0.05 and revenues of $0.12M, representing a 20% Y/Y increase. In the past three months, EPS estimates have seen one upward revision and none downward, while revenue forecasts have experienced two downward revisions and no upward changes.
  • Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) +3% - Shares climbed after a WSJ report said Paramount Skydance ((PSKY)), Netflix ((NFLX)), and Comcast ((CMCSA)) are preparing first-round, non-binding bids for the entertainment giant ahead of the Nov. 20 deadline. Paramount reportedly intends to bid for the entire company, while Netflix and Comcast are focused on acquiring the Warner Bros. studios and HBO Max. Paramount has already made three prior bids, most recently at $23.50/share, all of which were rejected. Last month, WBD launched a strategic review following multiple unsolicited offers for both the entire company and its studio/streaming units

Biggest stock losers

  • Fossil Group (FOSL) -10% - Shares slipped after reporting mixed Q3 results marked by a 6.1% drop in net sales to $270.2M, as weakness in DTC sales (-27%) and restructuring of its physical retail network outweighed a slight increase in wholesale revenue; softness continued across the Americas and Europe, while Asia posted a 2% gain. The company reiterated its FY2025 outlook for mid-teens sales declines and adjusted operating margin around breakeven to slightly positive. It also noted the completion of its previously announced debt exchange, extending maturities by three years and adding $32.5M in incremental financing.
  • HIVE Digital Technologies (HIVE) -5% - Crypto-linked stocks slid as Bitcoin (BTC-USD) dipped below $100,000, with investors pulling money from token-related ETFs amid a shift toward risk-off sentiment. Notable decliners included Strategy (MSTR) -6%, Circle Internet (CRCL) -3%, Riot Platforms (RIOT) -3%, MARA Holdings (MARA) -3%, Coinbase (COIN) -1%, Bit Digital (BTBT) -2%, and CleanSpark (CLSK) -4%.
  • Applied Materials (AMAT) -2% - Shares fell even as FQ4 results and its FY2026 outlook topped expectations. For FQ1, the company anticipated an adjusted EPS midpoint of $2.18 vs. $2.11 consensus and revenue between $6.35B and $7.35B, with the $6.85B midpoint above consensus. CFO Brice Hill said the company is positioning itself for notably stronger demand expected to materialize in the second half of calendar year 2026.

data.title: Kansas City Fed's Schmid argues against rate cuts, for smaller Fed balance sheet
data.description:
Kansas City Fed President Jeffrey Schmid, who voted against a 25 basis-point rate cut at the Federal Open Market Committee's last meeting, hasn't changed his mind. Easing policy further could keep inflation, now running at ~3%, above the central bank's target, while it wouldn't do much to shore up the labor market, he said in a speech on Friday.

"Cuts could have longer-lasting effects on inflation as our commitment to our 2% objective increasingly comes into question," he said, according to prepared text for the speech. "I do not think further cuts in interest rates will do much to patch over any cracks in the labor market — stresses that more likely than not arise from structural changes in technology and immigration policy."

"That continues to guide my thoughts as I head into the meeting in December," he added. Any decision in that meeting, though, will be informed by data gathering and discussion in the upcoming weeks.

Schmid also discussed the Fed's balance sheet. He said he supported the central bank's decision to stop shrinking its balance sheet as of Dec. 1, 2025, due to signs that banking reserves are no longer abundant.

"In the longer-term, I would like to operate with the smallest and least distortivebalance sheet that we can," Schmid said. "A large balance sheet increases the Fed’s footprint in financial markets, distorts the price of duration and the slope of the yield curve, and potentially blurs the line between monetary and fiscal policy."

Any further reduction in the balance sheet will require "careful analysis of bank reserve demand and what the Fed might do to offset some of this demand." He made a couple of suggestions to that end.

"One possibility is to ease access to Fed facilities that might serve as substitutes for reserve holdings, such as the Standing Repo Facility," he said. The standing repurchase, or repo, facility allows banks to sell Treasuries to the Fed, providing short-term cash, with the understanding that the banks will repurchase them the next day.

The Fed could also lower the interest rate it pays on reserves within the target band, Schmid said. Currently, that rate is 15 basis points above the bottom end of the band. "Lowering the rate within the band would allow more space for other interest rates to move before bumping up against the top of the band," he said. "This would allow for a greater range of private intermediation of reserve demand before the Fed would feel the need to take action."

Another way the Fed can reduce its footprint in financial markets, without changing the overall size of its balance sheet, is to shorten the duration of its assets. "Currently, our asset holdings are disproportionately weighted towards long-duration assets, which distorts the price of duration, lowering long-term rates and artificially flattening the yield curve. By shifting our holdings toward shorter-duration securities, we can decrease this distortion."

data.title: Union Pacific, Norfolk Southern shareholders approve $85B merger
data.description:
Union Pacific (UNP) and Norfolk Southern (NSC) shareholders have agreed to merge the two railroad operators in an $85M deal  that would create the first coast-to-coast U.S. railroad.

The deal must still be approved by the U.S. Surface Transportation Board before a merger can be completed. However, in August, President Trump removed the only member of the Surface Transportation Board who expressed concerns about further consolidation in the railroad industry, clearing the way for the merger to be approved by U.S. regulators.

With the shareholder vote assured, Union Pacific (UNP) CEO Jim Vena plans to file the formal merger application either in late November or early December to initiate the government review process.

Shares of both Norfolk Southern (NSC) and Union Pacific (UNP) are roughly unchanged in Friday’s trading session.

data.title: Tencent added as a new short idea at Hedgeye
data.description:
Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY) was added as a new short idea at Hedgeye with the potential for 29% downside.

"Tencent has now become one of the most consensus, crowded AI longs in China—and that is precisely why we are turning contrarian," Hedgeye analyst Felix Wang wrote in a note on Thursday. 

Tencent was previously a long idea at Hedgeye since August 2023. 

"Tencent’s Q3 topline and earnings beat masked several pressure points," Wang wrote. "Domestic gaming lost market share in Q3—during what is already a `video game winter.' We expect meaningful deceleration starting Q4 2025, with any potential reacceleration from the game pipeline not showing up until 2H 2026 due to deferred revenue accounting."

Shares of Tencent ticked lower by 0.8% on Friday.

"Tencent is widely perceived as China’s “AI dragon,” thanks to embedding Hunyuan/Yuanbao across its ecosystem," Wang added. "But AI growth expectations are too high and we are seeing a capex pullback. With constrained chip supply and slowing investment, Tencent may be near its AI capability ceiling for the foreseeable future."

data.title: Adnoc’s €11.7B Covestro deal wins conditional E.U. approval
data.description:
The European Union's competition regulator said Friday it conditionally cleared Abu Dhabi's state oil company ADNOC for its €11.7 billion (~$13.6 billion) bid for German chemical company Covestro (OTCPK:CVVTF) (OTCPK:COVTY).

The European Commission said ADNOC's offer to maintain Covestro’s (OTCPK:CVVTF) (OTCPK:COVTY) intellectual property in Europe as well as concessions on the company’s unlimited state guarantee from the U.A.E. settled its earlier concerns that its state subsidies could stifle competition.

The deal, one of the largest foreign takeovers of an E.U. company by a Persian Gulf state, has prompted concerns that ADNOC may be using state subsidies to acquire Covestro (OTCPK:CVVTF) (OTCPK:COVTY); an in-depth investigation began earlier this year under relatively new legislation designed to tackle what European officials see as distorting subsidies from foreign governments.

data.title: Strategy is acquiring 'quite a lot' of bitcoin, Saylor says, amid sell-off
data.description:
Michael Saylor said Friday that Strategy (MSTR), the bitcoin (BTC-USD) treasury giant and business analytics firm he co-founded, is buying "quite a lot" of the crypto rout intensifies.

"We're buying quite a lot," Saylor told CNBC in an interview, adding that Strategy (MSTR) will disclose its latest bitcoin (BTC-USD) purchases on Monday. He reassured that investors may be "pleasantly surprised," as "we've been accelerating our purchases."

The bitcoin (BTC-USD) evangelist's remarks come as BTC's price briefly fell to below $95K for the first time in about six months -- on the heels of sizable net outflows from spot bitcoin exchange-traded funds -- as a wave of risk aversion swept through global markets.

"We're always buying," Saylor told CNBC, underscoring his unwavering long-term confidence in BTC. "Bitcoin is always a good investment," but only if "you can handle the volatility" with long time horizons of at least four years.

As of Nov. 9, 2025, Strategy (MSTR) held 641,692 bitcoin (BTC-USD), valued at roughly $61B at current prices. MSTR's aggregate stash has an average purchase price of ~$74.1K, compared with ~$96.2K at press time.

data.title: LM Funding America targets efficiency gains with 70 petahash expansion and share buyback amid treasury growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: LM Funding America (LMFA) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Bruce Rodgers, Chairman, CEO & President, stated that the third quarter focused on "execution, integration and disciplined capital allocation, as we continue building LM Funding into a vertically integrated Bitcoin miner with a simple ambition: increase Bitcoin per share and grow intrinsic value over time." He highlighted the acquisition of an 11-megawatt facility in Columbus, Mississippi, expanding total capacity to 26 megawatts and diversifying operational risk. Rodgers reported, "By the end of September, we had integrated Mississippi, energized additional capacity and exited the month with approximately 304.5 Bitcoin in treasury, valued in at nearly $35 million versus a market capitalization of roughly half that amount."
  • Rodgers described a private securities repurchase that "retired more than 3.3 million shares and over 7.3 million warrants in a single transaction, reducing dilution, simplifying our capital structure and increasing our Bitcoin per share." He also announced a $1.5 million stock buyback and the deployment of Bitmain S21 immersion-cooled machines to further improve efficiency.
  • Ryan Duran, President of US Digital & Mining Co and Vice President of Operations, noted a "50% hash rate expansion in build cycle," with Mississippi adding 7.5 megawatts of energized capacity at a power cost of $0.036 per kilowatt hour. Duran said, "Bitcoin production increased 28% sequentially, rising from 5.9 Bitcoin in September to 7.6 Bitcoin in October."
  • Richard Russell, Chief Financial Officer, reported, "For the third quarter, revenue was $2.2 million, up approximately 13% sequentially and 74% year-over-year." Russell added, "Mining margins improved to 49%, driven by a shift from hosting fees to self-mining, utilizing our curtailment and energy sales to offset mining expenses and higher fleet efficiency." He said, "We reported a net loss of $3.7 million and a core EBITDA loss of $1.4 million, both driven by increased staff costs and payroll expenses."

Outlook

  • Management emphasized continuing the strategy to "balance Bitcoin accumulation, strategic investment and opportunistic share repurchases that we'll use only when it strengthens the balance sheet without sacrificing per share value." Rodgers reiterated, "Every action we take, every machine deployment, every site decision, every capital move is designed to improve per share ownership, per share cash flow and per share Bitcoin."
  • Duran projected that new Bitmain S21 immersion-cooled machines will "add roughly 70 petahash of compute power" and are scheduled to be energized in December, with expectations for a "step change in efficiency and should meaningfully increase Bitcoin per megawatt at the site."

Financial Results

  • Russell stated, "For the third quarter, revenue was $2.2 million, up approximately 13% sequentially and 74% year-over-year." Mining margins improved to 49%. Curtailment and energy sales totaled $152,000, down from $223,000 in Q2. The company reported a net loss of $3.7 million and a core EBITDA loss of $1.4 million, citing increased staff and payroll expenses as key drivers.
  • At quarter end, LM Funding held $300,000 in cash and cash equivalents and 304 Bitcoin valued at $34.7 million. As of October 31, the Bitcoin treasury stood at approximately 295 Bitcoin valued at $31.9 million or $2.62 per share.
  • Russell stated, "Our liquidity, treasury and credit capacity give us flexibility to support operations, growth and continued share repurchases while limiting dilution and preserving long-term upside for shareholders."

Q&A

  • Matthew Galinko, Maxim Group LLC: "With your mining infrastructure pretty radically different from where it was entering '25, I'm curious if you could maybe give us some thoughts on how you think about that your path in '26 as far as the Bitcoin mining infrastructure and equipment goes?" Rodgers replied, "The Mississippi acquisition has worked really, really well... we've got a nice runway there that we didn't anticipate. So I look for more growth there and on the magnitude of what we've achieved this year, it seems foreseeable."
  • Galinko followed up: "...you're not necessarily pursuing or close on any additional site acquisitions or is that something you're still exploring...?" Rodgers said, "We always have people exploring site acquisitions when we do it based on where the energy tariffs and then we look for a property that goes with those energy tariffs."
  • Galinko asked about capital allocation between mining and direct Bitcoin acquisition. Rodgers explained the company plays a long game, "looking at what do we think the price of Bitcoin is. And that means you don't necessarily make a dollar decision based on the current circumstances."
  • Sky Moore, for Kevin Dede, HCW: "...with about 15% of your old machines in storage... how are you guys managing your fleet... going forward?" Duran replied, "...having those machines in the wings is a great way to quickly deploy once that power becomes available."
  • Moore requested the current cost of mining one Bitcoin. Russell answered, "Our current mining costs right now per Bitcoin for this most recent quarter was $66,000. Last quarter, it was like $70,000."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed a positive to neutral tone, commending operational progress and raising questions on future growth, site acquisition, capital allocation, and cost structure.
  • Management adopted a confident and forward-looking tone, emphasizing operational achievements, capital discipline, and efficiency gains. Phrases such as "we are entering our next efficiency phase" and "every action we take... is designed to improve per share ownership" reflected conviction.
  • The tone was more constructive and detailed this quarter, with management providing deeper explanations and transparency around capital allocation, operational efficiency, and growth levers.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from focusing on M&A and immersion expansion in Q2 to emphasizing integration, efficiency, and per share economics in Q3.
  • Strategic focus moved from asset acquisition to capital structure optimization, share buybacks, and maximizing Bitcoin per share.
  • Analyst questions evolved from clarifications on asset deployment (Q2) to exploring the long-term capital allocation strategy and efficiency gains (Q3).
  • Key metric changes included sequential revenue growth, margin improvement, and a reduction in per Bitcoin mining costs from $70,000 to $66,000.
  • Management's tone was more assertive regarding treasury growth and operational leverage, while analysts maintained a constructive approach.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted the primary challenge remains in balancing capital allocation between mining expansion and outright Bitcoin purchases, as well as optimizing fleet deployment for maximum efficiency.
  • Analyst questions highlighted concerns about capital allocation, site acquisition opportunities, and cost containment in mining operations.
  • Russell highlighted staff and payroll expenses as drivers of the net loss, but emphasized the company's flexibility to support operations and growth through liquidity and credit capacity.

Final Takeaway

LM Funding America concentrated on scaling its vertically integrated mining operations, expanding capacity, and enhancing per share Bitcoin economics through disciplined capital allocation. The company executed a share and warrant repurchase, authorized a new buyback, and announced the addition of 70 petahash in immersion-cooled compute power. Management reported improved mining margins, lower per Bitcoin costs, and increased treasury value relative to market capitalization, underscoring a strategy focused on operational efficiency, shareholder alignment, and long-term Bitcoin accumulation, with an eye toward further efficiency and production gains in the coming quarters.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
